summary: DHH argues that letting tasks slide is not negligence but a rational acceptance of finite time and energy. With an essentially unlimited number of things that could be done, the difference between doing 8 or 12 tasks is a rounding error. He emphasizes that completed work creates more work — plans need updating, processes need monitoring — so doing less also reduces future burden. The post encourages people to stop apologizing for deliberately choosing not to do things and to accept that something bad will happen regardless.
key_points: The work is never done, so all you can control is what you're willing to let slide — and most days that should be most things
Completing tasks produces work products that must be maintained forever, making work a self-replicating process
Letting things slide is a rational acceptance of finite time, not a sign of neglect
There are always more good reasons not to do something than to do it
money_quotes: All you can control is what you're willing to let slide. Most days that should be most things.
Letting things slide is not a sign of neglect. It's an acceptance of our finite allotment of time, motivation, skill, and knowledge.
The difference between getting 8 or 12 things done in a day is not really a 50% increase in productivity, but a rounding error in the grand picture of completion.
The less you do, the less you accumulate.
Work begets work begets more work.
Stop apologizing for the deliberate act of letting it slide.
Something bad is bound to happen somewhere regardless of what you do.
key_insight: Deliberately letting most things slide is not failure but wisdom — completed work self-replicates, so doing less today means carrying less forever.
